did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used machine learning techniques, specifically logistic regression (LR), linear discriminant analysis (LDA), support vector machine (SVM), and Naive Bayes, to develop classification models for the diagnosis and differentiation of melanocytic skin tumors (melanoma vs. melanocytic naevus) using quantitative parameters from dermatoscopic, spectrophotometric, and ultrasonic images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the acquisition of diagnostic images from 100 patients (91 analyzed), extraction of quantitative parameters using Matlab, and development/validation of four machine learning classification models (LR, LDA, SVM, Naive Bayes) to differentiate melanocytic naevus from melanoma. It includes specific methods, results (accuracy >90%), and ethical approval details, characteristic of an original research study rather than a review.

title: Diagnostics of Melanocytic Skin Tumours by a Combination of Ultrasonic, Dermatoscopic and Spectrophotometric Image Parameters
brief_summary: This study developed a computer-aided diagnostic system to differentiate cutaneous melanoma from melanocytic naevus by combining quantitative parameters from optical dermatoscopy, spectrophotometry, and high-frequency ultrasonography. The researchers extracted informative features from these three imaging modalities and trained four machine learning classifiers: logistic regression, linear discriminant analysis, support vector machine, and Naive Bayes. The models were evaluated against histological examination results using metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and area under the ROC curve. The proposed hybrid technique achieved an accuracy of more than 90% for logistic regression, linear discriminant analysis, and support vector machine classifiers. Specifically, the support vector machine model demonstrated the highest performance with 98.9% accuracy, 100% specificity, and an AUROC of 0.999. The results indicate that combining these three non-invasive imaging techniques significantly improves diagnostic accuracy compared to using any two techniques alone.

lesion_types_studied.details: Malignant melanocytic tumor; 41 cases in the study cohort of 91 analyzed patients.
Benign melanocytic tumor; 50 cases in the study cohort of 91 analyzed patients.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of cutaneous melanoma (CM) versus melanocytic naevus (MN)
all_task_types.details: The paper develops four machine learning models (Logistic Regression, Linear Discriminant Analysis, Support Vector Machine, and Naive Bayes) to perform binary classification between two discrete diagnostic categories: cutaneous melanoma and melanocytic naevus. The models use quantitative parameters extracted from dermatoscopic, spectrophotometric, and ultrasonic images as input features. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, Matthews correlation coefficient, and AUROC.

datasets_used.name: In-house Clinical Cohort (Kaunas, Lithuania)
datasets_used.description: A proprietary dataset of diagnostic images (dermatoscopic, spectrophotometric, and high-frequency ultrasonic B-scan) acquired from patients at the Lithuanian University of Health Sciences and Ultrasound Research Institute, Kaunas University of Technology. Images were processed using Matlab to extract quantitative parameters for machine learning classification.

use.training: Used to train four machine learning classifiers (Logistic Regression, Linear Discriminant Analysis, Support Vector Machine, Naive Bayes) using extracted quantitative parameters.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the performance of the classifiers against histopathological ground truth using metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and AUROC.
use.other: Nine cases from an initial cohort of 100 patients were excluded due to artifacts within images.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To determine statistical significance of quantitative parameters for feature selection before classification.
To select parameters for input data for the binary classification algorithm.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in the quantitative parameter values between cutaneous melanoma (CM) and melanocytic naevus (MN) groups.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in the quantitative parameter values between cutaneous melanoma (CM) and melanocytic naevus (MN) groups.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Parameters with p < 0.05 were considered statistically significant and selected as input data for the binary classification algorithm; non-significant parameters were excluded.

Case 1: Dermatoscopy + Spectrophotometry.metric: Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, Precision, MCC, AUROC
Case 1: Dermatoscopy + Spectrophotometry.notes: SVM achieved the highest accuracy among all models for this combination. Accuracy was more than 74% for all classifiers.

Case 2: Dermatoscopy + Ultrasonic B-scan.metric: Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, Precision, MCC, AUROC
Case 2: Dermatoscopy + Ultrasonic B-scan.notes: SVM showed the highest accuracy (91.21%) and specificity (100%). Performance of LR and LDA was reduced compared to Case 1. Accuracy was more than 76% for all classifiers.

Case 3: Spectrophotometry + Ultrasonic B.metric: Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, Precision, MCC, AUROC
Case 3: Spectrophotometry + Ultrasonic B.notes: SVM achieved the highest performance with 95.60% accuracy and 0.912 MCC. More than 85% accuracy and sensitivity were achieved for LR, LDA, and SVM. Naive Bayes performance remained the lowest. AUROC for LDA (0.905) was slightly less than Case 1 (0.906).

Case 4: Combination of All Three Techniq.metric: Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, Precision, MCC, AUROC
Case 4: Combination of All Three Techniq.notes: The proposed hybrid technique combining all three modalities achieved the best results. SVM demonstrated the highest performance with 98.9% accuracy, 100% specificity, and an AUROC of 0.999. Accuracy of more than 90% was estimated for LR, LDA, and SVM.

architecture_used.main_model: Four traditional machine learning classifiers: Logistic Regression (LR), Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA), Support Vector Machine (SVM), and Naive Bayes.
architecture_used.input_size: null (Input consisted of selected quantitative parameters extracted from images, not raw image dimensions)

architecture_details.Logistic_Regression: Used Logit mechanism; predictor variables scale-dependent and quantitative.
architecture_details.Linear_Discriminant_Analysis: Aims to maximize the ratio of between-group variance to within-group variance.
architecture_details.Support_Vector_Machine: Utilized Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel for non-linear separation.
architecture_details.Naive_Bayes: Probabilistic classifier based on Bayes theorem with strong independence assumptions between features.

training_details.purpose: To build classifier models and compute optimized parameters during the training stage.
training_details.feature_selection: Parameters selected based on statistical significance (p < 0.05) using t-test.

preprocessing_techniques: Ultrasonic B-scan: Interpolation of A-scan signals to achieve four times oversampling and reduce distortions.
Ultrasonic B-scan: Manual selection of front skin surface detection limits and maximum depth for analysis.
Ultrasonic B-scan: Application of a second-order low-pass Butterworth filter for front and back surface contour following.
Ultrasonic B-scan: Optimal least-square polynomial approximation (1st to 7th order) for boundary detection.
Ultrasonic B-scan: Calculation of average RMS value of the B-scan spectrum.
Ultrasonic B-scan: Application of zero-phase digital filtering to smooth average RMS spectra.
Ultrasonic B-scan: Removal of front-surface reflections and reverberations (second-order reflections).
Ultrasonic B-scan: Application of thresholding via moving average filter rectification (absolute values > 0.7) to locate back-surface reflections.
Ultrasonic B-scan: Extraction of Region of Interest (ROI) by tracking front and back surface reflections from lesion boundaries.
Ultrasonic B-scan: Conversion of extracted ROI images to grayscale.
Ultrasonic B-scan: Calculation of energy and entropy from the histogram of grayscale images.
Ultrasonic B-scan: Computation of quantitative parameters including mean, standard deviation, RMS, variance, smoothness, skewness, and kurtosis.
Ultrasonic B-scan: Creation of grayscale co-occurrence matrix (GSCM) to compute texture features (contrast, energy, correlation, homogeneity).
Dermatoscopic and Spectrophotometric images: No initial preprocessing was performed.
conclusions: The proposed hybrid technique combining quantitative parameters from ultrasonic B-scan, dermatoscopic, and spectrophotometric images achieved reliable differentiation between melanocytic naevus and cutaneous melanoma.
The accuracy of the proposed method exceeded 90% for logistic regression (LR), linear discriminant analysis (LDA), and support vector machine (SVM) classifiers.

The support vector machine (SVM) classifier demonstrated the highest performance among the tested models, achieving 98.9% accuracy, 100% specificity, and an AUROC of 0.999 when all three imaging modalities were combined.
Combining all three imaging modalities (dermatoscopy, spectrophotometry, and ultrasonography) significantly improved diagnostic accuracy compared to using any two techniques alone.
The study confirms that integrating multiple non-invasive quantitative imaging techniques with machine learning can enhance the diagnostic accuracy for melanocytic skin tumors.
important_remarks: The study cohort is small (n=91 analyzed cases) and geographically limited to a single center in Kaunas, Lithuania, raising concerns about external validation and generalizability.
Nine cases were excluded from the initial cohort of 100 due to image artifacts, which may introduce selection bias.
The dataset is imbalanced with 50 melanocytic naevi and 41 cutaneous melanomas.

The reported performance metrics (e.g., SVM accuracy 98.9%, specificity 100%) are exceptionally high and may indicate overfitting given the small sample size and lack of an independent external test set.
Feature selection was performed using a t-test (p < 0.05) on the entire dataset before cross-validation, which constitutes data leakage and likely inflates performance estimates.
The study relies on traditional machine learning classifiers (LR, LDA, SVM, Naive Bayes) rather than deep learning, limiting its comparison to modern state-of-the-art CAD systems.
Ground truth was determined by histopathology confirmed by two dermatopathologists, with a third called for discrepancies, providing a robust reference standard.
